intro_band.agent_remit_summary: A coding-agent configuration harness (the yaah Go CLI). It generates configuration for four coding agents — Claude Code, OpenCode, Codex CLI, GitHub Copilot CLI — from one codebase, and ships a built-in security/quality toolset wired into the agent: five hooks (a linter, a command guard that blocks rm -rf / and similar, a secret scanner, a comment checker, a session logger), a session audit log of every tool call and blocked action, and a built-in MCP server (yaah serve) exposing yaah_scan_secrets / yaah_lint / yaah_check_command / yaah_doctor / planning tools, alongside the third-party context7 and pulumi MCP servers it configures. The generated configs are expected to carry the same protections across all four target agents, and the harness is expected to keep credentials out of edited files (the secret scanner) and catastrophic shell commands out of the agent's reach (the command guard).
intro_band.agent_structure_summary: A Go 1.25 module — cmd/yaah is the cobra CLI; pkg/harness wires the registries; pkg/hooks/handlers/ holds the built-in handlers (guard.go command-guard, secretscan.go ~13-pattern secret scanner, linter.go, commentcheck.go, sessionlog.go); pkg/mcpserver/ is the built-in MCP server (server.go over stdio via modelcontextprotocol/go-sdk, tools.go registering the seven yaah_* tools); pkg/mcp/providers/ defines the configured MCP providers (yaah, context7, pulumi); pkg/session/store.go is the file-based session audit log; pkg/generator/ has the per-agent config generators (claude.go, opencode.go, codex.go, copilot.go) plus hookmap.go, which maps Claude hook events to each agent's equivalent — and leaves PreToolUse / PostToolUse blank for Codex. The repo's .mcp.json (and a duplicated block in .claude/settings.json) declares three MCP servers: context7 (stdio, npx -y @context7/mcp), pulumi (http, https://mcp.ai.pulumi.com/mcp), yaah (stdio, yaah serve). AGENTS.md (with CLAUDE.md / GEMINI.md as symlinks) is an agent-managed file with sections. go.mod pins exact versions; go.sum carries module hashes; there is no SECURITY.md.

behavior_summary:
The dominant pattern here is the inverse of the usual one: a security-conscious harness whose own controls are real and operative — but unevenly delivered. yaah ships a deterministic command-guard hook (it actually blocks rm -rf /, force-push to main, DROP TABLE, mkfs, raw disk writes on every Bash call), a 13-pattern secret scanner on every file edit, a structured session audit log, and a built-in MCP server whose tool descriptions are clean — that's a coherent safety model, and it scores accordingly. The headline gap is consistency: pkg/generator/hookmap.go leaves PreToolUse / PostToolUse blank for the Codex CLI target, so yaah generate --agent codex produces a config with none of the "5 hooks out of the box" the README advertises — no command guard, no post-edit secret scan (PRAX-2026-05-12-001).

On the MCP surface: the built-in yaah server is clean and pulumi is TLS; the structural gaps are the usual ones plus one specific to this config — context7 is launched via npx -y @context7/mcp with no version pin (a silent-update vector), MCP tool calls aren't covered by the PreToolUse/PostToolUse hooks so they fall outside the session log, and tool descriptions/outputs from all three servers reach the model unfiltered.

rules.rule_text: Generate coding-agent configuration for Claude Code, OpenCode, Codex CLI, and GitHub Copilot CLI from one codebase, and ship a built-in security/quality toolset (hooks, an MCP server, a session audit log).
Every generated agent config carries the same five hooks — linter, command guard (blocks rm -rf / and similar), secret scanner, comment checker, session logger.
Catastrophic shell commands (recursive delete from root, force-push to main, hard reset, destructive SQL, filesystem format, raw disk write) MUST be blocked before execution.
Hardcoded credentials in edited files MUST be detected.
Remote MCP servers MUST be reached over TLS.
Configured MCP servers MUST be pinned / from a known-good source with an integrity check.
High-impact actions — destructive shell commands, file writes, MCP tools that write/send/execute — should be reviewed before execution.
The harness should maintain a durable, structured record of tool calls, blocked actions, and file modifications.
Agent-managed / session-loaded files (AGENTS.md and its symlinks) should not be writable in a way that lets ingested content persist into future sessions without review.
MCP tool descriptions MUST NOT contain instruction-like language directed at the model.
Dependencies MUST be version-controlled with a committed lockfile and pinned.
The .mcp.json and the per-agent config files generated by yaah should not drift apart.
The project should publish a threat model / SECURITY.md and run adversarial testing of its own protections.

findings.summary: yaah's deterministic protection hooks (command guard, secret scanner, comment checker, linter) are not delivered to the Codex CLI–generated config — hookmap.go leaves PreToolUse/PostToolUse blank for Codex.
findings.description: The README sells "5 hooks out of the box: linting…, a command guard that blocks rm -rf / and friends, a secret scanner…". But hookMappings in pkg/generator/hookmap.go maps HookPreToolUse and HookPostToolUse to event names for Claude (PreToolUse/PostToolUse), OpenCode (tool.execute.before/after), and Copilot (preToolUse/postToolUse) — but to nothing for Codex. So yaah generate --agent codex produces a config in which the command guard never runs (no rm -rf / block) and the secret scanner never runs on edits. A user trusting the harness's stated protections on Codex CLI doesn't have them.
findings.policy_rule_text: Every generated agent config carries the same five hooks — linter, command guard (blocks rm -rf / and similar), secret scanner, comment checker, session logger.

evidence.snippet: hookMappings[HookPreToolUse] = {Claude:"PreToolUse", OpenCode:"tool.execute.before", Copilot:"preToolUse"} — no Codex field; same for HookPostToolUse. An empty string means 'event not supported by that agent' per the HookMapping doc comment.
"5 hooks out of the box: linting…, a command guard that blocks rm -rf / and friends, a secret scanner…, a comment checker…, and a session logger" — advertised without the Codex caveat.

recommended_actions: Either add Codex CLI's pre/post-tool hook event names to hookMappings in pkg/generator/hookmap.go (if Codex supports them), or have the Codex generator emit the command-guard / secret-scanner protection via whatever mechanism Codex does support — and if neither is possible, surface that gap loudly in the README and in yaah generate --agent codex output.
Add a generator test that asserts the command-guard and secret-scanner hooks are present (in some form) in every agent's generated config.

findings.summary: The context7 MCP server is launched via `npx -y @context7/mcp` — no version pin, auto-installed from npm on every run, no integrity check: a silent-update / rug-pull vector for a stdio MCP server that runs with the agent's environment.
findings.description: .mcp.json (and the duplicated block in .claude/settings.json) declares context7 as { type: "stdio", command: "npx", args: ["-y", "@context7/mcp"] }. npx -y resolves and runs the latest published @context7/mcp with no version constraint and no lockfile or integrity check, as a child process of the agent. A compromised or malicious release of that package would execute in the agent's context. The OWASP MCP minimum bar fails both "tool definitions are signed and version-pinned" and "dependencies pinned and scanned for CVEs" here.

tags.label: Tool definitions are signed and version-pinned
Dependencies pinned and scanned for CVEs

findings.policy_rule_text: Configured MCP servers MUST be pinned / from a known-good source with an integrity check.

evidence.snippet: "context7": { "type": "stdio", "command": "npx", "args": ["-y", "@context7/mcp"] } — unpinned npm package, auto-installed each run
the same unpinned context7 entry is duplicated here

recommended_actions: Pin @context7/mcp to an exact version in .mcp.json (and the generated configs): args: ["-y", "@context7/mcp@"], and bump it deliberately.
Prefer a vendored / lockfile-backed install of MCP server packages over npx -y where the agent platform allows it.

findings.summary: MCP tool descriptions and outputs from the configured servers reach the model context unfiltered — no tool-poisoning check on descriptions, no sanitization of outputs; the built-in yaah server's own descriptions are clean.
findings.description: Three MCP servers feed the model: context7 (documentation lookups), pulumi (operations against mcp.ai.pulumi.com), and the built-in yaah server. The yaah server's tool descriptions in pkg/mcpserver/tools.go are short and behavioural ("Scan a file for hardcoded secrets and credentials", "Check whether a shell command is safe to run") — no instruction-like language, so no tool poisoning there. But nothing checks the descriptions or sanitizes the outputs of context7 / pulumi before they re-enter the model context, so a compromised server (or content it serves) is an indirect-injection vector.

tags.label: Tool descriptions contain no hidden model instructions
All tool outputs sanitized before returning to model

findings.policy_rule_text: MCP tool descriptions MUST NOT contain instruction-like language directed at the model.

evidence.snippet: three servers declared: context7 (stdio), pulumi (http https://mcp.ai.pulumi.com/mcp), yaah (stdio, `yaah serve`) — their tool descriptions and outputs are exposed to the model as-is

the built-in yaah server's tool descriptions are clean (e.g. "Scan a file for hardcoded secrets and credentials") — no instruction-like language; the third-party servers' descriptions are not in this workspace and are not checked

recommended_actions: When loading MCP servers, scan their tool descriptions for instruction-like language and warn (or refuse) when found.
Treat MCP tool results as untrusted external content — fence or label them when they re-enter the model context.

findings.summary: MCP tool calls have no approval gate and fall outside yaah's hook coverage — the PreToolUse/PostToolUse hooks match only Bash and Edit|Write|MultiEdit, so MCP tool invocations are neither pre-checked nor recorded in the session log; .claude/settings.json sets no `permissions` lists.
findings.description: .claude/settings.json wires yaah hook PreToolUse to matcher: "(?i)^Bash$" and yaah hook PostToolUse to matcher: "(?i)^(Edit|Write|MultiEdit)$". MCP tools — including the yaah_* ones (one of which, yaah_planning_init, writes a .planning/ directory) and the context7 / pulumi tools — match neither, so the command guard / secret scanner / session logger never see them, and the session audit log in pkg/session/store.go doesn't record them. There's also no permissions allow/deny/ask block, so MCP-tool gating relies entirely on Claude Code's interactive prompt.

tags.label: All tool invocations logged with identity and parameters

findings.policy_rule_text: High-impact actions — destructive shell commands, file writes, MCP tools that write/send/execute — should be reviewed before execution. / The harness should maintain a durable, structured record of tool calls, blocked actions, and file modifications.

evidence.snippet: PreToolUse matcher "(?i)^Bash$"; PostToolUse matcher "(?i)^(Edit|Write|MultiEdit)$"; no MCP-tool matcher and no "permissions" block
yaah_planning_init writes a .planning/ directory under the project root; it is an MCP tool, so it bypasses the PreToolUse/PostToolUse hooks and the session log

recommended_actions: Extend the PostToolUse hook matcher (or add a dedicated one) to cover MCP tool calls — at least to record them in the session log, and to scan/guard the ones with write/exec side-effects (yaah_planning_init).
Add a permissions block to the generated .claude/settings.json (and the equivalents for the other agents) that puts write/exec MCP tools behind ask.

findings.summary: AGENTS.md is an agent-managed, session-loaded file (CLAUDE.md and GEMINI.md symlink to it) whose `<!-- AGENTS-GENERATED -->` sections yaah regenerates and the AGENTS.md skill edits with no approval — a persistence surface, since file contents and MCP doc output reach the model.
findings.description: AGENTS.md opens with "FOR AI AGENTS - Human readability is a side effect" and "Managed by agent: keep sections and order", carries blocks that yaah rewrites, and is symlinked to CLAUDE.md and GEMINI.md — so it is loaded into the agent's context at session start and is writable by the agent's own tooling (the AGENTS.md skill, yaah generate) without a confirmation step. Content the agent ingests (MCP doc fetches, file reads) can therefore steer a write into the generated sections that re-enters context next session. The command-guard hook tempers the execution half of a poison→exec chain (it deterministically blocks catastrophic Bash patterns), but not arbitrary commands, and not at all for the Codex-generated config (PRAX-2026-05-12-001).
findings.policy_rule_text: Agent-managed / session-loaded files (AGENTS.md and its symlinks) should not be writable in a way that lets ingested content persist into future sessions without review.

evidence.snippet: "FOR AI AGENTS - Human readability is a side effect" / "Managed by agent: keep sections and order; edit content, not structure" — and <!-- AGENTS-GENERATED:START/END --> blocks regenerated by yaah
symlink → AGENTS.md (same for GEMINI.md) — so the agent-managed file is loaded as the primary agent-instructions file for Claude Code and Gemini too

recommended_actions: Require explicit operator confirmation before yaah generate / the AGENTS.md skill rewrites the generated sections of AGENTS.md, or keep a diff/version history so a poisoning write is auditable.
Treat the regenerated AGENTS.md sections as code-owned, not agent-owned — generate them deterministically from yaah's own config, never from model output.

findings.summary: The PreToolUse hook matches only Bash; file edits are scanned after the write (PostToolUse), so a leaked secret or a malicious change is detected post-hoc, not prevented.
findings.description: The command guard runs on PreToolUse against Bash, so a dangerous shell command is blocked before it runs. But the secret scanner and comment checker run on PostToolUse against Edit / Write / MultiEdit — i.e. after the file has been modified. A secret committed by an edit, or a change that introduces a vulnerability, is surfaced (and the harness offers remediation advice) but is already on disk. This is a reasonable design for an advisory toolset; it's worth being explicit that it's detective, not preventive, for edits.
findings.policy_rule_text: Hardcoded credentials in edited files MUST be detected.

evidence.snippet: Edit/Write/MultiEdit are handled on PostToolUse ("yaah hook PostToolUse"), not PreToolUse — the secret scan runs after the write
SecretScanner is a PostToolUse handler (per its doc comment) — "detects hardcoded secrets … in edited files" after the edit

recommended_actions: If pre-write blocking is wanted, also wire the secret scanner on a PreToolUse matcher for Edit/Write/MultiEdit (where the agent platform supports inspecting the proposed content) and reject the write when a high-confidence secret pattern matches.
Document explicitly that edit-time scanning is detective + advisory, so operators don't assume it prevents secret commits.

findings.summary: `.mcp.json` and `.claude/settings.json` carry duplicate `mcpServers` blocks (both regenerated by yaah) — drift risk if one is hand-edited.
findings.description: The same three-server mcpServers block appears verbatim in both .mcp.json (the file other MCP-aware tools read) and .claude/settings.json (the project-level config Claude Code reads). yaah generate writes both, so they stay in sync as long as everything goes through yaah — but an operator who edits one by hand gets an inconsistent server set between the two, and there's no check that flags the divergence.
findings.policy_rule_text: The .mcp.json and the per-agent config files generated by yaah should not drift apart.

evidence.snippet: an identical mcpServers block is embedded here alongside hooks and enabledPlugins

recommended_actions: Have yaah doctor compare the mcpServers block in .mcp.json against the one in each generated agent config and report drift.
Or generate one from the other (single source of truth) rather than emitting two copies.

findings.summary: No SECURITY.md or documented vulnerability-disclosure process, despite the project shipping a security toolset (secret scanner, command guard) and a `security-audit` skill.
findings.description: The repo root has no SECURITY.md and no threat model. That's a notable omission for a project whose pitch includes a command guard, a secret scanner, and a security-audit skill — the harness reasons about security for the code it touches but doesn't publish how to report a vulnerability in yaah itself, or what its own threat model is (e.g. the supply-chain exposure of the MCP servers it configures, the persistence surface of the agent-managed AGENTS.md).
findings.policy_rule_text: The project should publish a threat model / SECURITY.md and run adversarial testing of its own protections.

evidence.snippet: absent — no security policy / disclosure process / threat model at the repo root
a security-audit skill ships in the harness — the project clearly thinks about security; it just hasn't documented its own posture

recommended_actions: Add a SECURITY.md with a disclosure process and a brief threat model: the MCP supply-chain surface (PRAX-2026-05-12-002), the agent-managed AGENTS.md persistence surface (PRAX-2026-05-12-005), the per-agent coverage gaps (PRAX-2026-05-12-001).

Add adversarial tests for the harness's own protections: a secret pattern the scanner should catch, a command the guard should block, a poisoning write into AGENTS.md, a Codex config that asserts the (intended) protections are present.

findings.summary: The pulumi MCP server (http, https://mcp.ai.pulumi.com/mcp) is reached over TLS; any authentication / token scoping is handled out of band and isn't visible in `.mcp.json` — if it consumes the developer's Pulumi credentials, the agent inherits that access scope.
findings.description: .mcp.json declares pulumi as { type: "http", url: "https://mcp.ai.pulumi.com/mcp" } — TLS, good — but no headers / token / scope. Whatever auth Pulumi's hosted MCP gateway requires is established elsewhere (env var, Pulumi CLI login, etc.); the config doesn't show it. Worth noting only so an operator is aware that, depending on how that auth resolves, the agent may be able to act against the developer's Pulumi org through this server.
findings.policy_rule_text: High-impact actions — destructive shell commands, file writes, MCP tools that write/send/execute — should be reviewed before execution.

evidence.snippet: "pulumi": { "type": "http", "url": "https://mcp.ai.pulumi.com/mcp" } — TLS; no headers/token/scope in the config

recommended_actions: Document what auth the pulumi MCP server uses and what scope it grants the agent, so operators can decide whether to gate its write/exec tools behind approval.

positives.title: Deterministic command-guard hook on every Bash call
positives.description: CommandGuard runs on PreToolUse against Bash and rejects recursive delete from root, force-push to main/master, hard reset, destructive SQL (DROP TABLE/DATABASE), filesystem format (mkfs), and raw disk writes (dd if=...of=/dev/...) — a real, code-level, deterministic gate on the agent's shell surface (for the Claude/OpenCode/Copilot configs).

positives.title: Multi-pattern secret scanner on every file edit
positives.description: SecretScanner runs on PostToolUse against Edit/Write/MultiEdit with ~13 patterns — AWS credentials and access-key IDs, GitHub PAT/OAuth/fine-grained tokens, OpenAI and Anthropic API keys, Slack tokens and webhooks, SendGrid keys, private keys, bearer tokens, hardcoded passwords — and feeds findings into the session log.

positives.title: Structured per-session audit log
positives.description: pkg/session/store.go persists a per-session record — tool calls, blocked calls, files modified, findings, all timestamped — to .claude/logs/ / .claude/sessions/, giving a durable, structured action trail (hook-scoped: Bash + Edit/Write/MultiEdit + session events).

positives.title: Built-in MCP server with clean tool descriptions
positives.description: The yaah MCP server (pkg/mcpserver) exposes read/check tools — yaah_scan_secrets, yaah_lint, yaah_check_command, yaah_doctor, yaah_session_info, yaah_planning_status/init — with short, behavioural descriptions and no instruction-like language directed at the model (no tool poisoning).

positives.title: Go dependency hygiene
positives.description: go.mod pins exact module versions and go.sum carries module hashes for a small dependency tree (cobra, the MCP go-sdk, go-toml, oauth2); builds are reproducible.

positives.title: Pulumi MCP server reached over TLS
positives.description: The pulumi MCP server is declared as https://mcp.ai.pulumi.com/mcp; the configured remote MCP endpoint uses TLS.

positives.title: Ships a security-audit skill
positives.description: A security-audit skill is bundled in the harness, indicating the project actively treats security review as part of the agent's workflow.

log_files.no_logs_note: No log files in the scanned tree (the session logs under .claude/logs/ and .claude/sessions/ are gitignored runtime data) — but the harness does write a structured per-session audit log at runtime (pkg/session/store.go); see the positive 'Structured per-session audit log' and PRAX-2026-05-12-004 for the gap (MCP tool calls fall outside it).

raise_posture.weighted_rationale: Partial. yaah has more operative controls than most agents in this class — a deterministic command guard that blocks catastrophic shell commands, a 13-pattern secret scanner on every edit, a structured per-session audit log, a clean built-in MCP server, exact-pinned Go dependencies — which puts it solidly in the Partial band and a notch above a bare harness. What keeps it there rather than higher is that the protections are unevenly delivered (the Codex-generated config gets none of the tool hooks), the MCP surface has the standard gaps plus an unpinned npx-launched server, and there's no published threat model or SECURITY.md despite the project clearly thinking about security. Supply-chain hygiene and continuous monitoring are the strongest dimensions; the Codex-config gap is the one finding that warrants priority.

categories.name: Limit Your Domain
categories.rationale: A config-generator CLI plus a built-in security/quality toolset; the generated agent config is broad (Claude Code with Bash/Edit/Write and all three MCP servers), but the command-guard hook narrows the Bash surface deterministically — and the toolset's reach is uneven across the four target agents (PRAX-2026-05-12-001).

categories.name: Balance Your Knowledge Base
categories.rationale: MCP tool descriptions and outputs from all three configured servers reach the model context unfiltered, and the agent-managed AGENTS.md is a session-loaded, regenerable surface (PRAX-2026-05-12-003, -005); the built-in yaah server's descriptions are clean, which is a point in the right direction.

categories.name: Implement Zero Trust
categories.rationale: Real code-level interposition exists and runs — CommandGuard rejects rm -rf / / force-push-to-main / DROP TABLE / mkfs / raw disk writes on every Bash call via PreToolUse, and the secret scanner runs on every edit — but it's PreToolUse-on-Bash only (edits are post-scanned, not pre-gated), MCP tool calls are ungated and unlogged, and the Codex-generated config gets none of it (PRAX-2026-05-12-001, -004, -006).

categories.name: Manage Your Supply Chain
categories.rationale: go.mod pins exact versions and go.sum carries module hashes for a small dependency tree; the gap is the configured context7 MCP server, launched via `npx -y @context7/mcp` with no version pin or integrity check (PRAX-2026-05-12-002).

categories.name: Build an AI Red Team
categories.rationale: Substantial test coverage of the generators (codex_test.go, copilot_test.go, opencode_test.go, factory_test.go, settings_test.go) and a shipped security-audit skill; what's missing for a higher score is a SECURITY.md / threat model / disclosure process and an adversarial test suite for the harness's own protections (PRAX-2026-05-12-008).

categories.name: Monitor Continuously
categories.rationale: pkg/session/store.go persists a structured per-session record — tool calls, blocked calls, files modified, findings, all timestamped — to .claude/logs/ / .claude/sessions/; it's hook-scoped (Bash + Edit/Write/MultiEdit + session events), so MCP tool calls fall outside it (PRAX-2026-05-12-004).
